title: Lathyrus linnaei
intro: Lathyrus linnaei, also known as the hairy vetch or woolly vetch, is a perennial legume plant native to Europe, Asia, and Africa. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and diuretic effects, making it beneficial in treating conditions such as arthritis, edema, and urinary tract infections. The bioactive constituents of Lathyrus linnaei include flavonoids, alkaloids, and saponins, which contribute to its therapeutic properties. Herbal preparations made from Lathyrus linnaei include infusions, tinctures, and decoctions, which are commonly used in traditional medicine to treat various ailments.
taxonomy: Lathyrus linnaei is a species of flowering plant in the pea family Fabaceae. It is a type of vetch or pea, with the specific classification being Lathyrus linnaei. The genus Lathyrus is a group of flowering plants that are native to the Northern Hemisphere, and Lathyrus linnaei is one of the species within this genus. The classification of Lathyrus linnaei can be broken down further into the kingdom Plantae, phylum Magnoliophyta, class Magnoliopsida, order Fabales, family Fabaceae, genus Lathyrus, and species L. linnaei. This classification helps to understand the evolutionary relationships and characteristics of the plant.

side_effects: Lathyrus linnaei, also known as the sweet pea, contains a toxin called ODAP (oxalyldiaminopropionic acid) in its seeds. Consuming large amounts of these seeds can cause severe health issues, including neurolathyrism, a condition characterized by paralysis, muscle weakness, and spasticity. Neurolathyrism can be permanent and is often irreversible. The risk of developing this condition is higher in populations that rely heavily on Lathyrus linnaei seeds as a food source. It's essential to consume the seeds in moderation, if at all, and to avoid them during periods of food scarcity.
